original_abstract: Bisphenol A (BPA) is a known endocrine disruptor found in many consumer products that humans come into contact with on a daily basis. Due to increasing concerns about the safety of BPA and the introduction of new legislation restricting its use, industry has responded by adopting new, less studied BPA analogues that have similar polymer-forming properties. Some BPA analogues have already been shown to exhibit effects similar to BPA, for example, contributing to endocrine disruption through agonistic or antagonistic behaviour at various nuclear receptors such as estrogen (ER), androgen (AR), glucocor- ticoid (GR), aryl hydrocarbon (AhR), and pregnane X receptor (PXR). Since the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) issued a draft re-evaluation of BPA and drastically reduced the temporary tolerable daily intake (t-TDI) of BPA from 4 mg/ kg body weight/day to 0.2 ng/kg body weight/day due to increasing concern about the toxic properties of BPA, including its potential to disrupt immune system processes, we conducted a comprehensive review of the immunomodulatory activity of environmentally abundant BPA analogues. The results of the review suggest that BPA analogues may affect both the innate and acquired immune systems and can contribute to various immune-mediated conditions such as hypersensitivity reactions, allergies, and disruption of the human microbiome.
humanized_abstract: Abstract The endocrine disrupting chemical Bisphenol A (BPA) is one of the most studied chemicals due to its wide distribution in human everyday life. In response to the negative publicity and legislation restricting the use of BPA, manufacturers have switched to less well studied BPA alternative plastics. Some of these analogues have similar properties to BPA and have been shown to interact at similar sites on nuclear receptors thereby causing similar endocrine disrupting effects to BPA. These effects include binding with agonistic or antagonistic effects to estrogen (ER), androgen (AR), glucocor- ticoid (GR), aryl hydrocarbon (AhR), and pregnane X receptor (PXR) receptors. The European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) recently published a draft assessment for Bisphenol A (BPA) following a request by the European Commission to reassess the safe level of BPA for humans. The safe level was drastically lowered from 4 mg/kg body weight/day to 0.2 ng/kg body weight/day due to adverse effects that have been reported such as immune system disruption. As a result, we assessed the current knowledge on the immunomodulatory effects of BPA alternatives plastics. Our analysis suggests that BPA alternatives may affect the innate and acquired immune responses and may therefore lead to adverse health effects such as type I hypersensitivity reactions, allergies or disruption of the human gut microbiota.
